pages.title: Welcome to Social Engineering Awareness
pages.subtitle: Learn how attackers manipulate people, not just systems, to gain access to information, accounts, and physical spaces.

pages.title: What is social engineering?
pages.text: Social engineering is the use of manipulation, deception, or pressure to trick people into revealing confidential information or taking unsafe actions. Instead of breaking into a system through technical means, attackers often try to exploit trust, fear, urgency, curiosity, or helpfulness. This can happen through email, phone calls, text messages, fake websites, in-person conversations, or physical access attempts.

pages.title: Why attackers target people
pages.text:
People are often the easiest path into an organization.

An attacker may not need advanced hacking tools if they can convince someone to:

• share a password
• click a malicious link
• open an infected attachment
• approve a login request
• hold open a secure door

Attackers know that employees want to be helpful, responsive, and cooperative. Social engineering takes advantage of those normal behaviors.

image.alt: Why attackers target people instead of systems

pages.title: Common manipulation tactics
pages.text: Social engineering often relies on emotional pressure or false trust. Common tactics include: • Urgency – "Do this right now." • Authority – "I’m from IT, HR, or management." • Fear – "Your account will be locked." • Curiosity – "Open this file to see the update." • Helpfulness – "Can you help me real quick?" A message or person may sound believable while still being unsafe.

image.alt: Examples of urgency, authority, fear, and curiosity

pages.title: Pretexting and fake authority
pages.text:
Pretexting happens when someone creates a believable story to gain your trust.

Examples include:

• someone claiming to be from IT and asking for your password
• a caller pretending to be a vendor needing account details
• a person claiming they were told to pick up records or equipment

Always verify requests for sensitive information through official channels before taking action.

image.alt: Example of fake authority or pretexting

pages.title: Tailgating and physical access
pages.text: Social engineering is not limited to email or phone scams. Tailgating happens when an unauthorized person follows an employee into a restricted area without proper access. This may happen when someone says they forgot their badge, are in a hurry, or are delivering something. Do not assume someone belongs in a secure area just because they act confident or familiar.

image.alt: Unauthorized person entering behind an employee

pages.title: Baiting and curiosity traps
pages.text:
Attackers may also use baiting to tempt someone into unsafe action.

Examples include:

• a USB drive left in a parking lot or lobby
• a file labeled "Payroll" or "Confidential"
• an unexpected attachment that looks important
• a free download or reward that requires login information

Curiosity is normal, but unknown files, devices, or downloads should never be trusted automatically.

image.alt: Examples of baiting and curiosity-based attacks

pages.title: What to do when something feels off
pages.text: If a request feels unusual, rushed, or manipulative: 1) Pause before acting 2) Verify the request through official channels 3) Do not share passwords, MFA codes, or confidential data 4) Do not grant access to secure spaces without authorization 5) Report suspicious behavior using your organization’s process It is always better to verify than to guess wrong.

image.alt: Safe response steps for suspicious requests

pages.title: Ready for the quiz?
pages.subtitle: This short quiz will test your understanding of common social engineering tactics.
